Discord .well-known Domain Verification Doesn't Work on GitHub Pages — Here's the Fix

I tried to set up Discord's HTTPS domain verification on my GitHub Pages-hosted Hexo blog and hit a wall — GitHub Pages flat-out ignores .well-known directories. This post walks through what went wrong, why Hexo also needed a tweak to even generate the .well-known folder, and the workaround that actually got Discord to verify: spinning up a dedicated .well-known repo as a separate GitHub Pages site.
Monorepo? Another Way to Manage Projects — Building a Vue3 Monorepo With Vite + pnpm

What is a Monorepo? How is it different from a regular project setup? I had a need for one recently, so I wrote this up. The post walks through Monolith vs Multi vs Monorepo, plus a hands-on Vue3 Monorepo build using Vite + pnpm.
Tired of Windows 10 Forcing Updates on You? Here's How to Disable Auto-Updates

Windows 10's forced update behavior is the worst — slowing your machine mid-work, throwing random new bugs, or worst of all bluescreen on a botched update. This post walks through disabling the Windows Update service from the Services UI. Heads up — this also disables security updates, so only do it if you understand the security trade-off.
GitHub Pages Lets You Set Up Your Own 404 Page Too

GitHub Pages actually supports a custom 404 page out of the box — no extra config needed. This post explains what a 404 page is for (UX and SEO), what GitHub Pages' default 404 looks like, and how to drop a 404.html (or 404.md with permalink) into your repo root to ship your own.
"Localhost Refused to Connect" on Taiwan's HiCOS Citizen Certificate? Here's the Fix

If you're trying to use Taiwan's Citizen Digital Certificate on a government site and your browser keeps showing "localhost refused to connect" — usually the problem is ChkSrv.exe not running in the background. This post walks through how to fix it.
Why You Should Understand Memory Leaks (memory leak)

"Memory" is a word that matters to every developer — how memory gets used directly affects how your program runs. This post walks through what memory leaks are, how garbage collection actually works, and three common patterns in JavaScript that quietly leak memory: forgotten event listeners, runaway timers, and closures that hold onto more than they should.
Why My Blog Wouldn't Update: A Service Workers Stale-Cache Story

Readers kept telling me my blog hadn't been updated in ages — but I was posting regularly. After some digging it turned out to be a Service Workers issue left over from when I'd dropped PWA from my blog. This post walks through how I diagnosed it, how to manually unregister Service Workers in Chrome DevTools, and how to auto-unregister them for visitors so they don't get stuck on a stale cache.
What Is Encryption? Why Do We Encrypt? A Beginner's Take

Something came up recently that made me want to write a beginner-level post on encryption. This one covers what encryption actually is, why we use it, the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption (with examples like AES, RSA, and how GitHub SSH works), and a section on Base64 — which a lot of people mistake for encryption but is actually just encoding.
Working in the Gambling Industry as an Engineer: A Reality Check

The gambling industry feels mysterious to outsiders — high salaries and an opaque vibe make people curious, but the same factors make it scary. I worked in this space briefly (in Taiwan), and several friends still do. This post shares what I learned about the tech stacks, the lifestyle, the comp, and the real risks — including legal exposure that's specific to Taiwan but worth thinking about no matter where you work.
The Light and Shadow of Frontend Engineering: Think It Through Before You Switch In

We're in an age where information is everywhere and easy to grab — which means we have to spend more energy thinking, comparing, and deciding. Especially when "becoming a developer" is the trendy career switch. This post is the honest version of what frontend engineering looks like — the upside (salary ceiling, opportunities, flexibility) and the downside (information anxiety, communication load, the reality of "remote" and "freedom") — so you can make an informed decision.
